title.title: How Smart Contracts Work
title.subtitle: Workflow, EVM, Web3 landscape, gas economics, and why you should build with them

gasExercise.heading: Gas Estimation Challenge
gasExercise.instructionA: Click the 5 operations in order from
gasExercise.explanation: Storage writes are expensive because they modify global state on every full node permanently.

ops.label: Read a public variable
ops.detail: View call — never hits the chain

ops.label: Emit an event
ops.detail: Logged on-chain but not in state

ops.label: Transfer ETH (simple send)
ops.detail: Base cost for moving ETH between accounts

ops.label: Write to a new storage slot
ops.detail: Most expensive regular operation on the EVM

ops.label: Deploy a new contract
ops.detail: Pays for every byte of bytecode stored

stackExercise.heading: Build the dApp Stack
stackExercise.promptIdle: Click a component, then click its layer to place it.
stackExercise.promptSelectedA: Now click a layer to place
stackExercise.infoHovered: Hover any placed component to see what it is.
stackExercise.infoIdle: Click (or hover) a component to read what it does, then click a layer to place it.

frontend.label: Frontend Layer
frontend.desc: What the user sees and interacts with

metamask.label: MetaMask
metamask.info: A browser-extension wallet that stores your keys and signs transactions for the user. It runs entirely in the frontend and never custodies funds on a server.

react.label: React / Next.js
react.info: The UI framework that renders the dApp interface in the browser. It holds no on-chain logic — it just calls contracts through a wallet or RPC.

infura.label: Infura RPC
infura.info: A hosted RPC gateway that lets apps read state and broadcast transactions without running their own node. It exposes the blockchain over a normal HTTPS endpoint.

solidity.label: Solidity Contract
solidity.info: The smart-contract source code that defines the dApp's on-chain rules and state. Once compiled and deployed, it lives on-chain and runs on every node.

evm.label: EVM
evm.info: The Ethereum Virtual Machine executes contract bytecode deterministically. It is the runtime baked into every node, so all of them reach the same result.

pos.label: PoS Validators
pos.info: Validators stake ETH to propose and attest blocks under Proof of Stake. They are the on-chain consensus layer that orders and finalises transactions.

ipfs.label: IPFS
ipfs.info: A peer-to-peer network for storing large files like images and metadata off-chain. Contracts keep only a content hash, so on-chain storage stays cheap.

thegraph.label: The Graph
thegraph.info: An indexing protocol that turns raw on-chain events into fast, queryable APIs. It runs off-chain so frontends read data without scanning the whole chain.

chainlink.label: Chainlink Oracle
chainlink.info: An oracle network that delivers external data such as prices and randomness onto the chain. It bridges off-chain information into contracts that otherwise can't reach it.

web3.heading: The Web3 Landscape
web3.subtitle: Not just a technology shift — a paradigm shift in how trust is established.

before.title: Trust intermediaries
before.note: You trust that the intermediary is honest, solvent, and won't be hacked or censored.

after.title: Trust mathematics and transparent code
after.note: You trust the protocol — not the people running it.

implication.title: Implication: Disintermediation
implication.body: When trust is enforced by code rather than institutions, middlemen become optional. Smart contracts can replace escrow agents, brokers, settlement systems, and notaries — automatically, 24/7, globally.

buildingBlocks.label: dApps
buildingBlocks.desc: Front-ends that interact with smart contracts via wallets

buildingBlocks.label: Smart Contracts
buildingBlocks.desc: Business logic deployed on-chain, permanently executable

buildingBlocks.label: Blockchain
buildingBlocks.desc: Shared ledger and VM — Ethereum, Solana, BNB Chain…

buildingBlocks.label: Wallets
buildingBlocks.desc: Identity + private key management — your passport to Web3

buildingBlocks.label: Oracles
buildingBlocks.desc: Bridges between on-chain logic and real-world data

dapp.heading: dApps & Smart Contracts
dapp.subtitle: A dApp is not just a smart contract — it's a full stack where blockchain is one layer.

pillars.title: Traditional Frontend
pillars.subtitle: Web or mobile interface
items: React / Next.js / Vue — standard web stack
RPC endpoints: query blockchain state (Infura, Alchemy)

pillars.title: Off-Chain Components
pillars.subtitle: Infrastructure outside the blockchain
items: Storage: IPFS, Arweave (large files, NFT metadata)
Indexers: The Graph — query blockchain data like a DB
APIs: oracles, payment rails, notification services

dapp.stackAlt: dApp stack: User → Browser → Web Server (Front-end) → Smart Contracts → Ethereum Virtual Machine → Ethereum Blockchain
dapp.insightBody: only the blockchain layer is decentralized. Most dApps still rely on centralized hosting (Vercel, AWS) for their frontend — a nuance that's often overlooked.

vsApp.heading: The App: Web2 vs Web3
vsApp.subtitle: The same product, built on two very different foundations.

rows.b: No one can block valid txs

vsApp.takeaway: a dApp trades a company you must trust for a protocol anyone can verify — at the cost of paying gas per action.

vsContract.heading: The Agreement: Traditional vs Smart Contract
vsContract.subtitle: Same intent — enforced by people and courts, or by code.
vsContract.takeaway: a smart contract removes lawyers, delay and ambiguity — but only for logic that can be expressed in code.

standards.heading: Token Standards
standards.subtitleB: — an agreed set of functions a token promises to have, so any wallet, exchange or app can use it with zero custom code.

erc20.title: Fungible — every unit identical
erc20.desc: Money-like tokens. 1 unit = 1 unit — always interchangeable.

erc721.title: Non-fungible — each one unique
erc721.desc: One-of-a-kind tokens. Each has a unique ID and owner — not interchangeable.

erc1155.title: Multi-token — both, one contract
erc1155.descA: One contract holds many token types — fungible

standards.footerA: MetaMask can show any ERC-20 balance and OpenSea any ERC-721 — without knowing the project — because they all expose the

components.heading: Anatomy of a Smart Contract
components.subtitle: Every smart contract is made of five building blocks — understanding them is understanding the language of Web3.

cards.title: Code
cards.desc: The business logic and conditional statements. Written in Solidity (Ethereum), Rust (Solana), or Vyper. Once deployed, the code is immutable.

cards.title: State
cards.desc: Stored data that the contract maintains and modifies over time. Examples: token balances, ownership records, vote counts. Persisted on-chain.

cards.title: Functions
cards.desc: Specific operations callable by external parties or other contracts. Can be read-only (free) or state-changing (costs gas).

cards.title: Events
cards.desc: Logs that record important contract activities. Emitted when key actions occur — cheaply stored and readable by off-chain systems.

cards.title: Blockchain Platform
cards.desc: The execution environment. Ethereum, Polygon, Solana, BNB Chain… Each has different performance, cost, and tooling trade-offs.

components.platformBody: this Solidity contract deploys to Ethereum, Polygon, Arbitrum, Base, and any EVM-compatible chain with zero code changes.

workflow.heading: How Smart Contracts Work
workflow.subtitle: Five steps from deployment to notification — all on-chain, all automatic.

steps.label: Deploy
steps.desc: Contract code is compiled and uploaded to the blockchain. It gets a permanent address — immutable from this point on.

steps.label: Trigger
steps.desc: A user or system sends a transaction to the contract address, calling a function with the required inputs.

steps.label: Execute
steps.desc: The EVM (or equivalent VM) runs the contract code on every node in the network simultaneously.

steps.label: Update
steps.desc: State changes are recorded on the blockchain — permanent, transparent, and agreed upon by consensus.

steps.label: Emit
steps.desc: Events are emitted and logged on-chain, notifying off-chain systems (front-ends, indexers, oracles).

solidity.heading: Reading a Smart Contract
solidity.subtitle: You don't need to write Solidity — but every PM, auditor, and analyst must be able to read it. Here is a minimal ERC-20 token contract, fully annotated.

annotations.title: Contract Declaration
annotations.desc: Like a class in OOP. All state and functions live inside. Once deployed, this code is immutable at its address on the blockchain.

annotations.title: State Variables
annotations.desc: Stored permanently on-chain. public creates a getter function automatically. Every write costs gas — reads are free.

annotations.title: Mapping (Key→Value Store)
annotations.desc: mapping(address => uint256) is the on-chain equivalent of a database table. Every Ethereum address maps to a token balance. Not iterable — you must know the key.

annotations.title: Event
annotations.desc: Logged to the blockchain but NOT stored in state. Cheap to emit (~375 gas). Indexed fields enable efficient off-chain search. This is how block explorers display token transfers.

annotations.title: Constructor
annotations.desc: Runs exactly once at deployment. Sets the token name, symbol, and total supply. After this, it never runs again — state is locked as deployed.

annotations.title: msg.sender
annotations.desc: The address that called this function — in the constructor, this is the deployer. All initial supply goes to them. A critical security variable: always validate who msg.sender is.

annotations.title: Function
annotations.desc: external means only outside callers can call this (not the contract itself). public allows both external and internal calls. Every function that changes state costs gas.

annotations.title: require (Guard Clause)
annotations.desc: If the condition is false, the entire transaction reverts — no state changes, no gas refund for execution so far. This is the Checks step in Checks-Effects-Interactions.

execution.heading: Execution Environment
execution.subtitle: Why smart contracts run the same way, everywhere, every time.

properties.title: Virtual Machines
properties.subtitle: Isolated execution environments
properties.desc: The EVM (Ethereum), SVM (Solana), WASM (Polkadot, Near)… Each VM is a sandboxed environment that cannot access the host machine's filesystem, network, or memory. Code runs in isolation — securely and predictably.

properties.title: Distributed Execution
properties.subtitle: Same code runs on thousands of nodes
properties.desc: When a transaction triggers a smart contract, every full node in the network runs the same code independently. There is no single server — execution is replicated across the globe.
examples: Redundancy eliminates single points of failure
No one can selectively block execution

properties.title: Deterministic Results
properties.subtitle: Identical inputs always produce identical outputs
properties.desc: Smart contracts cannot use randomness, real-time clocks, or external data without special tools. This constraint is what makes distributed consensus possible — every node must agree on the result.

properties.title: Consensus Requirement
properties.subtitle: Majority agreement required for state changes
properties.desc: After execution, nodes compare results. Only if a supermajority agrees on the output does the state change get written to the blockchain. A single malicious node cannot affect the outcome.
examples: PoS: validators attest to execution results

evmCore.title: Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)
evmCore.desc: The original standard — runs Solidity & Vyper bytecode

execution.deployBody: EVM-compatible chains share the same bytecode standard — one Solidity contract works across 30+ chains.

capabilities.heading: New Capabilities
capabilities.subtitle: Five things smart contracts make possible that no prior technology could.

items.title: Permissionless Composability
items.tagline: Any contract can plug into any other — no API keys, no approval.
items.example: Flash loan: borrow $10M across 3 protocols and repay, in one atomic tx.

items.title: Atomic Transactions
items.tagline: Multi-step operations fully succeed or fully revert — never half-done.
items.example: DEX swap: send ETH → check liquidity → transfer tokens — all or nothing.

items.title: Global Shared State
items.tagline: One synchronized state, readable worldwide, with no central server.
items.example: An NFT's owner is instantly visible to every marketplace and game everywhere.

items.title: Programmable Money
items.tagline: Value with built-in rules — "pay only if delivery is confirmed."
items.example: Superfluid streams salary by the second — no payroll department.

items.title: Censorship Resistance
items.tagline: No government, company, or dev can block a valid transaction.
items.example: OFAC blacklisted Tornado Cash — the front-end died, the contract ran on.

why.heading: Why Build Using Smart Contracts?
why.subtitle: The case for choosing smart contracts over traditional software.

core.title: Eliminate Intermediaries
core.desc: No bank, notary, or escrow agent needed. The contract enforces the rules and releases funds automatically — reducing cost and counterparty risk.

core.title: Global by Default
core.desc: Deploy once, accessible to anyone on Earth with an internet connection. No geographic restrictions, no foreign exchange friction, no banking requirements.

core.title: Radical Transparency
core.desc: Every rule is public, readable, and auditable. Users don't have to trust a company's promises — they can verify the code themselves.

core.title: Always-On Automation
core.desc: Code executes the moment conditions are met — no office hours, no approval queues, no human latency. Payouts, transfers, and state changes happen in seconds.

impact.title: Programmable Money
impact.desc: Revenue splits, royalties, and conditional payments execute automatically on every transaction. Superfluid streams salary per second. Mirror splits publication revenue by ownership.

impact.title: Composable Ecosystem
impact.desc: Your contract can instantly integrate with Uniswap, Aave, Chainlink — no partnerships, no API keys. This "money lego" model accelerates product development exponentially.

impact.title: Immutable Commitments
impact.desc: Once deployed, the rules cannot be changed by any party — not even you. This removes the risk of unilateral platform changes that destroy user trust overnight.

impact.title: DAO Governance
impact.desc: Smart contracts enable token-weighted voting, on-chain treasuries, and automatic execution of governance proposals — organisations without a legal entity or board.

gas.heading: Gas & Transaction Economics
gas.subtitle: Every computation costs gas. Understanding this is essential for building real applications.

fundamentals.def: Unit measuring the computational work required for an operation. Every EVM opcode has a fixed gas cost — storing data costs more than reading it.
Market-determined cost per gas unit (denominated in Gwei — 1 ETH = 1,000,000,000 Gwei). Set by the user; higher price = faster inclusion.
Maximum gas the user is willing to spend on this transaction. Acts as a safety cap — prevents runaway loops from draining your wallet.
Gas Used × Gas Price = Total Cost in ETH. Unused gas (up to the limit) is refunded. You pay for what the EVM actually executes.

incentives.why: Pay for the computation and storage they consume — no free lunch, prevents spam.
Receive fees for processing transactions — economic incentive to keep the network running.
Higher fees during congestion create natural throttling — the market self-regulates throughput.
Base fee is burned, not paid to validators. Deflationary pressure on ETH supply during high usage.

impact.label: Congestion spikes
impact.desc: Network congestion can increase costs 10–100×. During the 2021 NFT boom, simple transfers cost $50–200.

impact.label: Speed vs. cost
impact.desc: Users set gas price: higher = faster inclusion, lower = wait. Wallets show estimated confirmation times.

impact.label: Layer 2 savings
impact.desc: Arbitrum, Optimism, Base reduce costs by 10–1000×. The same transaction that costs $5 on L1 costs $0.01 on L2.

reshape.heading: How Smart Contracts Reshape Traditional Business
reshape.subtitle: Five structural shifts that distinguish smart-contract-native businesses from traditional ones.
reshape.imgAlt: Two people exchanging documents directly — disintermediation

cards.title: Disintermediation
cards.desc: Peer-to-peer execution. No banks, brokers, or escrow services needed for the agreement to clear.

cards.title: Process Automation
cards.desc: Autonomous "if X, then Y" rules execute the moment conditions are met — no manual approval step.

cards.title: Trustless & Transparent
cards.desc: Trust shifts from people to code + consensus. All actions are verifiable, auditable, and global.

cards.title: Cost Reduction
cards.desc: Eliminates middlemen, manual paperwork, and ongoing legal oversight. Cuts escrow, reconciliation, and compliance costs.

cards.title: New Value Chains
cards.desc: Entirely new business models, products, and ecosystems become possible — DAOs, tokenized assets, M2M payments.

reshape.patternBody: traditional businesses centralize trust in an intermediary. Smart-contract-native businesses distribute that trust into code + consensus, then build new economic primitives on top — composability, atomicity, and global reach. The next section explores where this works in practice.

quiz.question: In the smart contract execution workflow, what triggers a contract to run?
options: A scheduled cron job on the blockchain's servers
A transaction sent to the contract's address on the blockchain
The developer manually calling the contract from their machine
The Ethereum Foundation approving the execution request
quiz.explanation: Smart contracts are passive — they sit on the blockchain and do nothing until triggered. A transaction sent to the contract address kicks off execution on the EVM. The contract then runs, updates state, and emits events — all within that single transaction.

takeaways.title: Section 02 — Key Takeaways
items: Smart contracts run inside a deterministic virtual machine (EVM) — every node gets the same result
Core components: state variables, functions, events, modifiers, and ABI
Web3 dApps combine a front-end with a wallet and on-chain smart contract logic
Gas is the cost of computation — every operation has a price in ETH
Smart contracts enable programmable money, automated settlement, and trustless governance

cards.summary: Stateless vs stateful · Trustless vs trusted server · Public ledger vs private database

cards.title: Why Build with SC?
cards.summary: Use when: multiple parties + conditional logic + no trusted intermediary + need for auditability
